description: Overview of the fundamental concepts of physical load and performance: physiological effects, fatigue, regeneration, overtraining, and performance measurement.

questions.text: What is the effect of physical load on the body?
questions.explanation: Physical load triggers physiological changes in the body (e.g., increases in heart rate, breathing rate, and metabolic activity) that lead to adaptation.

options.text: It triggers physiological changes in the body
It has no effect on the body
It reduces the work of the heart

questions.explanation: True. Fatigue is a reduction in the muscles' capacity to produce work, which has a direct negative impact on sports performance.
Regeneration is the body's process of returning to its pre-training state and preparing for the next training session.

options.text: The intense finishing phase of a training session
The body's return to its pre-training state

questions.text: Which of the following is the most characteristic sign of overtraining?
questions.explanation: Persistent performance decline despite adequate rest is the classic sign of overtraining syndrome.

options.text: A decrease in heart rate during exercise

questions.text: What is the purpose of performance measurement?
questions.explanation: The purpose of performance measurement is to objectively determine the athlete's current condition so that the training plan can be modified in a targeted manner.

options.text: To penalise the athlete for a poor result
To objectively determine the athlete's current condition
To guarantee the prediction of competition results
To decide when to end a training session

questions.text: Adequate rest is an integral part of the training plan.
questions.explanation: True. Rest is an essential component of the training process; without it, adaptation and development cannot occur.
Performance is determined by both inherited traits (genetics) and acquired fitness — not by either factor alone.

options.text: Both inherited traits and acquired fitness

questions.text: Overload can lead to injury in the long term.
questions.explanation: True. Chronic overload — when the body does not receive sufficient recovery time — leads to injury and the development of overtraining syndrome.

questions.text: What is the difference between fatigue and overtraining?
questions.explanation: Fatigue is a short-term condition that resolves with one or two days of rest. Overtraining is a prolonged state that is difficult to reverse over weeks or months.

options.text: There is no difference between them
Overtraining is a prolonged, harder-to-reverse condition
Fatigue is more serious than overtraining

questions.text: Performance is determined exclusively by genetic factors.
questions.explanation: False. Although genetics play a role, fitness level, nutrition, sleep, and mental preparation are equally determining factors.
